planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; not suitable for zone 9 and warmer where summer heat causes crown rot.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; dead nettle is one of the most effective and ornamental shade groundcovers for zones 3–8, valued for its silvery-marked, heart-shaped leaves that brighten dark corners throughout the growing season and its whorls of pink or white flowers in late spring and early summer. It is a rapid spreader that forms a dense, weed-suppressing carpet and is easy to propagate by stem cuttings or division. Unlike the weedy annual red dead nettle (L. purpureum), this perennial species is non-invasive and stays where it is planted. Named cultivars like 'White Nancy' (white flowers, heavily silver leaves), 'Beacon Silver', and 'Purple Dragon' are widely available. In the South, plant in a cool, shaded spot and provide afternoon shade from heat; it goes dormant and may die back in high summer in zone 8.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in any well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; once established, epimedium is remarkably drought tolerant and can thrive in the dry root zone under large trees.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; epimedium is among the most valuable groundcovers for difficult dry shade — one of the few plants that genuinely thrives in the dry, root-filled zone under mature maples, beeches, and other large shade trees where almost nothing else survives. The heart-shaped, often evergreen or semi-evergreen leaves are attractive year-round, turn bronze in winter, and the small but intricate crimson and pink bicolored flowers in early spring are delightful at close range before most shade perennials emerge. It spreads slowly but persistently by rhizome to form a dense, tidy, weed-suppressing mat. Shear back old foliage in late winter before new growth and flowers emerge. The genus contains dozens of outstanding species and cultivars; E. × rubrum is one of the most widely available and adaptable.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; slower to establish than Japanese pachysandra but more ecologically valuable and better adapted to southern heat.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Allegheny spurge is the native North American alternative to the commonly used Japanese pachysandra (P. terminalis), naturally found on the wooded slopes and ravines of the Appalachian mountains and Southeast. The large, scallop-edged leaves are semi-evergreen to evergreen and often show attractive silver marbling; fragrant white flower spikes in early spring attract early native bees. It performs better in zone 8–9 summer heat than Japanese pachysandra, is less prone to disease, and supports native insects — making it a superior choice in every way for southern shade gardens. Slower to spread than Japanese pachysandra but ultimately forms a dense, attractive groundcover. Never seen spreading invasively.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after last frost in very well-drained, lean soil in full sun; excellent drainage is essential — blue daze rots in moist, heavy soil. In zone 9–11 it acts as a perennial.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in very well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun after last frost; blue daze is one of the most beloved summer annuals in Gulf Coast gardens, producing a continuous cascade of brilliant sky-blue flowers from spring through frost over trailing stems of soft silver-green leaves. It behaves as a perennial groundcover in zone 9–10, spreading to form a low, drought-tolerant mat of near-constant bloom through the summer months when most plants struggle with heat. The blue flower color is exceptionally vivid under southern summer sun. Excellent in containers, hanging baskets, rock gardens, or as a front-of-border annual where summer heat and excellent drainage are provided. Propagate by rooted stem cuttings in fall to maintain plants through winter in zone 9+.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; in zone 8 it may die back to roots in hard freezes but re-sprouts in spring.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; Mexican sage is a large, bold, shrubby species with broad, deeply veined, highly aromatic leaves and long wands of blue-purple flowers from late summer through fall that attract hummingbirds and bumblebees. It is native to mountain forests and canyon edges in central Mexico at moderate elevation and tolerates partial shade better than most sages, making it useful in the lightly shaded areas of a Gulf Coast or zone 8–9 garden. The foliage has a strong, pleasant herbal fragrance when brushed and is deer resistant. It grows rapidly to a large sub-shrub in one season and may be damaged or killed by hard freezes but typically re-sprouts from the root crown in zone 8. Shear back by half in mid-spring to encourage compact, heavily blooming growth.
